guards.name: Closed Guard 🔒
guards.description: The closed guard is one of the fundamental positions in Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu. It involves wrapping your legs around your opponent's waist while lying on your back, creating a tight control that prevents them from passing.
execution: Lie on your back with your opponent between your legs
Lock your ankles behind their back at the lower back
Use your legs to break their posture by pulling them down
Control their sleeves or collar with your hands for grip control
Keep your hips active and mobile - never flat
Squeeze your knees together to prevent space
Keep your shoulders slightly off the mat to stay active
Use angle changes by moving your hips side to side
Maintain constant pressure with your legs to control distance
pros: Excellent control over opponent's posture and movement
Multiple submission opportunities from secure position
Prevents opponent from advancing position effectively
Great for beginners to learn fundamental guard concepts
Allows you to rest while opponent works
Safe position to attack from with low risk
Works well in self-defense scenarios
cons: Can be difficult against standing opponents
Uses significant energy to maintain against strong opponents
Less effective in No-Gi due to lack of grips
Limited mobility compared to open guards
Can be stalled by defensive opponents
Vulnerable to guard breaks from experienced passers
Restricted attacking angles compared to open guard
tips: Always break your opponent's posture first - this is priority #1
Use angle changes to create submission opportunities
Don't let your opponent establish a strong base or posture
Practice hip escapes to maintain and adjust the guard
Open your guard strategically to transition, not passively
Combine attacks - if one fails, chain to another immediately
Use overhooks and underhooks to control their arms
Keep your guard high on their back for better control
Don't cross your ankles when in mount - only in guard
Practice breaking grips to prevent guard opening

guards.name: Spider Guard 🕷️
guards.description: Spider guard is an open guard position where you control your opponent's sleeves while placing your feet on their biceps or inside their elbows, creating distance and controlling their posture.
execution: Start from open guard position with hips mobile
Grab both of your opponent's sleeves with strong grips
Place your feet on their biceps, pushing them away
Extend your legs to create tension and stretch them
Keep your hips off the mat and ready to angle
Use one foot to control the hip if doing single spider
Keep constant pressure and movement - never stay static
Combine with collar or pant grips for hybrid positions
Stay ready to transition to lasso, collar sleeve, or other guards
pros: Excellent distance control and management
Multiple sweep opportunities from every angle
Great for controlling aggressive opponents
Allows for transitions to other guards seamlessly
Prevents opponent from establishing strong base
Works well against heavier opponents using their weight
Creates opportunities for sweeps even when they stand
cons: Requires strong grip endurance and forearm strength
Only effective in Gi (sleeves required)
Can be passed with good balance and posture
Difficult against standing leg drag passes
Grips can be broken with proper grip fighting
Energy intensive to maintain for long periods
Vulnerable to stack passes if they get close
tips: Always maintain sleeve grips - if broken, re-establish immediately
Combine with lasso or collar guards for hybrid attacks
Keep your opponent off balance with constant pushing and pulling
Use bicep control to prevent them standing tall
Switch between single and double spider dynamically
Don't just push - pull with grips to create off-balance
Practice grip endurance with specific training
Chain your sweeps - if one fails, flow to another
Use spider to set up triangles and omoplatas

guards.name: De La Riva 🪝
guards.description: The De La Riva guard is named after Ricardo De La Riva. It involves hooking your opponent's leg from the outside with your leg while controlling their opposite sleeve, creating an asymmetrical control position.
execution: From open guard, identify opponent's lead leg
Hook your outside leg behind their near leg (shin to calf)
Wrap your foot around to their inner thigh for maximum control
Grip their opposite collar or sleeve with a strong grip
Use your other leg to control their hip or bicep
Keep your hips off the mat and mobile
Maintain constant tension pulling them forward
Stay active and create angles by scooting your hips
Keep your shoulders off the mat to prevent being flattened
pros: Excellent for off-balancing opponents
Multiple sweep combinations available
Great for transitioning to other guards
Works well against standing passes
Allows for advanced techniques like berimbolo and back takes
Effective in both Gi and No-Gi (with modifications)
Creates asymmetrical control that disrupts opponent's balance
cons: Complex for beginners to master
Requires good flexibility in the hips
Can be countered by backstep passes
Vulnerable to knee slice if not maintained properly
Requires constant movement and energy
Can be smashed if you get flattened
Leg positioning can be fatiguing to maintain
tips: Keep constant tension on the hook - never let it go slack
Combine with berimbolo attacks for modern game
Control the far sleeve to prevent defensive frames
Practice inversions for advanced attacks and scrambles
Chain your attacks - if sweep fails, transition to another guard
Keep your free leg active - use it to push, frame, or hook
Don't stay static - constantly shift angles and grips
Master the basic DLR sweep before moving to berimbolo
Use collar drag to set up back takes
Practice the kiss of the dragon for advanced back takes

guards.name: Butterfly Guard 🦋
guards.description: Butterfly guard is a seated guard where you use hooks under your opponent's legs while sitting up, allowing for dynamic sweeps and transitions.
execution: Sit with your knees bent and feet inside
Place your feet (hooks) under opponent's thighs
Keep your hooks active with feet flexed and engaged
Control their upper body with collar/sleeve grips
Keep your chest close to theirs
Stay on the balls of your feet ready to elevate
Keep your hips mobile and ready to shift angles
Use underhooks when possible
Create frames with your forearms to manage distance
pros: Powerful sweeps available
Works in both Gi and No-Gi
Great for smaller practitioners
Easy to stand up from
Excellent for entering leg entanglements (especially in no-gi)
Natural position for arm drags and back takes
Low energy expenditure compared to closed guard
cons: Requires awareness of back exposure
Requires good timing
Can be smashed by heavy pressure
Struggles against strong crossface pressure
Can be passed with stack passes if too upright
tips: Get underhooks whenever possible
Elevate and off-balance constantly
Chain your sweeps - if one fails, flow to another
Control the inside space - don't let opponent get double underhooks
Combine with half guard transitions
Practice the butterfly sweep mechanics
Use butterfly guard to stand up in scrambles
Practice your sit-up sweep as a high-percentage option
Drill transitions to X-guard and single leg X

guards.name: Half Guard 🛡️
guards.description: Half guard is a position where you control one of your opponent's legs between both of yours while on your back. It's a versatile position that bridges the gap between full guard retention and being passed, offering numerous sweeps and submission opportunities.
execution: Lie on your side with opponent's leg trapped between yours
Control their trapped leg with both your legs locked
Get an underhook on their far side when possible
Keep your inside knee blocking their hip
Stay on your side - never flat on your back
Control their upper body with frames or grips
Keep your head off the mat to prevent crossface
Use your bottom leg to control their ankle/knee
Maintain active hips ready to shift and create angles
pros: Great recovery position when guard is being passed
Works equally well in Gi and No-Gi
Excellent for smaller practitioners against bigger opponents
Multiple sweep and back take opportunities
Can transition to deep half, knee shield, or lockdown variations
Allows defensive players to slow the pace
Natural position for leg lock entries in modern game
cons: Can be smashed by heavy pressure if flat
Vulnerable to crossface and shoulder pressure
Requires constant battle for underhook
Can be difficult to attack if opponent establishes strong base
Risk of being passed if not active
Can be stalled by defensive top players
Requires good timing and technique to sweep effectively
tips: Get the underhook - this is the most important battle
Never let them flatten you - stay on your side
Use knee shield to create distance when needed
Practice deep half guard for modern sweeps
Combine half guard with butterfly hooks for hybrid attacks
Don't stay static - constantly shift between variations
Learn the lockdown for control in Gi and No-Gi
Practice coming up on your elbow/hand for better base
Study the old school sweep as high-percentage option
Chain your attacks - sweep to pass to submission

guards.name: X-Guard ✖️
guards.description: X-Guard is a powerful sweeping position where you control your opponent with both legs forming an 'X' under their body. One leg hooks behind their knee while the other controls their hip, creating excellent elevation and off-balancing opportunities.
execution: Start from open guard or half guard position
Get one foot on their hip (top hook)
Thread your other leg behind their near knee (bottom hook)
Create an X formation with your legs under their body
Control their sleeve or wrist on the trapped leg side
Keep your hips off the mat and mobile
Use your top hook to elevate and control balance
Pull their upper body forward to break posture
Stay ready to transition to single leg X or back take
pros: Extremely powerful sweeping position
Works excellently in both Gi and No-Gi
Great for smaller practitioners against bigger opponents
Natural transition to back takes
Excellent for standing up in scrambles
Multiple sweep angles available
Difficult for opponent to establish base or pressure
cons: Requires good timing to enter the position
Can be vulnerable to guard jumps and knee bars
Requires flexibility in the hips
Can be difficult against opponents who widen their base
Exposed to leg locks if you don't know defense
Requires constant movement and energy
Can be passed if opponent sits or drops weight correctly
tips: Master the entry from half guard and butterfly guard
Always control their upper body - pull them forward
Use your top hook to elevate constantly
Practice the back take as your primary option
Don't stay static - keep elevating and off-balancing
Chain X-guard with single leg X for more options
Study Marcelo Garcia's X-guard system extensively
Learn to stand up from X-guard in scrambles
Practice the technical stand-up sweep
Combine with butterfly hooks for hybrid sweeps

guards.name: Single Leg X 🦵
guards.description: Single Leg X (also known as Ashi Garami or Single Leg X-Guard) is a versatile open guard position where you control one of your opponent's legs using both of your legs — one hook behind the knee and the other foot on the hip. It is a cornerstone of modern leg lock systems and an excellent sweeping position.
execution: Start from open guard, butterfly guard, or X-guard
Control one of your opponent's legs with both of yours
Place your outside foot on their far hip for distance control
Thread your inside leg behind their near knee as a hook
Grip their ankle or heel on the controlled leg side
Keep your hips close to their leg and off the mat
Use your hip hook foot to push and create off-balance
Control their posture by pulling their upper body forward
Stay mobile and ready to transition to full X-guard or leg entries
pros: Foundation of modern leg lock systems (heel hooks, knee bars)
Works extremely well in both Gi and No-Gi
Excellent sweeping position with multiple angles
Easy to enter from many common positions
Great for smaller grapplers against bigger opponents
Allows seamless transitions to X-guard, back takes, and stand-ups
Low risk position when proper defense is maintained
cons: Exposes your own legs to counter leg locks
Can be passed if opponent frees their leg and applies pressure
Requires good hip mobility and leg dexterity
Vulnerable to smash passes if opponent drops weight
Needs constant engagement — static play gets shut down
Difficult to maintain against opponents with strong base
Learning curve for leg lock entries and transitions
tips: Always control their ankle or heel — never let the leg float free
Use your hip hook to constantly off-balance your opponent
Drill entries from butterfly, half guard, and standing
Chain single leg X with full X-guard for layered attacks
Study the Danaher Death Squad leg lock system for advanced entries
Practice the stand-up sweep — it's simple and high-percentage
Keep your inside hook tight behind their knee at all times
Learn proper leg lock defense before attacking from here
Combine sweeps with immediate leg lock attacks for dilemmas
Use angle changes to prevent your opponent from stacking you

guards.name: Deep Half Guard 🕳️
guards.description: Deep Half Guard is a bottom position where you scoot deep underneath your opponent's hips from half guard, controlling one of their legs while your body is almost entirely beneath them. It neutralizes their weight and creates powerful sweeping and back-take opportunities.
execution: Start from standard half guard with an underhook or overhook
Scoot your hips deep underneath your opponent's center of gravity
Control their far leg by hugging it tight to your chest
Get your head past their hip line on the far side
Keep your body perpendicular to theirs underneath them
Use your top arm to hug their thigh or control their belt/waistband
Frame with your bottom arm to prevent crossface or sprawl
Keep your inside hook (half guard hook) to prevent them stepping over
Stay tight and glued to their body — space is the enemy
pros: Neutralizes top player's weight and pressure effectively
Excellent sweeping position with high-percentage options
Works well for smaller grapplers against bigger opponents
Gives you initiative from a traditionally defensive position
Great transitions to back takes and single legs
Effective in both Gi and No-Gi
Difficult for opponent to submit you from deep half
cons: Vulnerable to d'arce and anaconda chokes during entry
Can be flattened if you don't get deep enough
Requires good timing on entries to avoid getting smashed
Opponent can use crossface to prevent you from getting deep
Risk of back exposure if sweep attempts fail
Guillotine choke risk if head positioning is poor
Can be stalled by experienced top players who sprawl and wait
tips: Commit fully when entering — halfway in deep half is dangerous
Always protect your neck during entries to avoid chokes
Hug their thigh tight like your life depends on it
Use the Homer Simpson sweep as your bread and butter
Study Jeff Glover and Bernardo Faria's deep half systems
Practice the Waiter Sweep for a high-percentage finish
Keep your hips moving and adjust angle constantly
Use deep half as a rest position — your opponent carries their own weight
Drill entries from standard half guard and quarter guard
Learn to chain sweeps — if one fails, immediately go to the next

guards.name: Rubber Guard 🧘
guards.description: Rubber Guard is a flexible closed guard variation popularized by Eddie Bravo and the 10th Planet Jiu-Jitsu system. It uses your own leg, pulled high with an overhook grip, to control the opponent's posture without relying on a gi. The position requires significant hip flexibility and is a cornerstone of no-gi guard play.
execution: Start from closed guard with your opponent in your guard
Break their posture down — this is essential before anything else
Overhook one of their arms and control their wrist on the other side
Bring your leg up high on the same side as the overhook
Grab your own shin or ankle and pull it across their back (Mission Control)
Keep your foot past their shoulder line for maximum control
Use the leg to keep their posture broken without using arms
Free your hands to set up attacks while your leg controls posture
Stay active with hip movement and transition through rubber guard positions
pros: Excellent posture control without needing gi grips
Frees up both hands for attacks while leg controls posture
Highly effective in MMA and No-Gi grappling
Creates powerful submission chains from bottom
Difficult for opponent to posture up or escape
Neutralizes size and strength advantages with technique
Offers unique attacks not found in traditional guard systems
cons: Requires above-average hip flexibility
Can cause knee and hip injuries if flexibility is insufficient
Steep learning curve with many unique position names
Less effective in Gi where opponents have more grips
Vulnerable to stacking if posture control is lost
Limited sweep options compared to other guards
Can be shut down by opponents who refuse to engage in guard
tips: Stretch daily — hip flexibility is non-negotiable for rubber guard
Master Mission Control before moving to advanced positions
Always break posture first — rubber guard without posture control fails
Learn the 10th Planet naming system (Zombie, Chill Dog, Crackhead, etc.)
Study Eddie Bravo's Mastering the Rubber Guard book and instructionals
Practice the omoplata as your primary attack from rubber guard
Use the Dead Orchard / Carni sequence for gogoplata entries
Chain attacks — if they defend one, flow to the next
Focus on the overhook side — it's the key to the whole system
Work on retaining Mission Control even when they try to posture

guards.name: Reverse De La Riva 🔄
guards.description: Reverse De La Riva (RDLR) is an open guard position where you hook your outside leg around the opponent's lead leg from the inside, with your shin behind their knee. Unlike standard De La Riva which hooks from the outside, RDLR hooks from the inside, making it an excellent guard for inverting, taking the back, and transitioning to berimbolo and other modern guard techniques.
execution: Start from open guard with your opponent standing or on one knee
Hook your outside foot behind their lead knee from the inside
Keep your shin across the back of their knee — this is the RDLR hook
Use your other foot on their hip or bicep for distance control
Grip their sleeve or collar on the hooked-leg side
Keep your hips off the mat and angled toward the hooked leg
Use your free hand to grip their belt, pants, or ankle
Stay ready to invert or transition to berimbolo
Maintain constant tension on the hook to prevent them stepping free
pros: Gateway to berimbolo and modern back take systems
Excellent for inverting and taking the back
Works well in both Gi and No-Gi
Great counter to knee slice and pressure passing
Allows seamless transitions to deep half, X-guard, and 50/50
Effective for lighter grapplers against heavier opponents
Creates many off-balancing and sweep opportunities
cons: Requires comfort with inverting and being upside down
Can be smashed if opponent drops heavy crossface pressure
Steep learning curve for berimbolo entries
Vulnerable to leg drag passes if hook is cleared
Requires strong hip flexibility and mobility
Can be countered by backstep to leg lock entries
Less intuitive than standard De La Riva for beginners
tips: Master the berimbolo entry — it's the signature attack from RDLR
Always keep tension on the hook — a loose hook gets passed easily
Study the Miyao Brothers for elite RDLR and berimbolo play
Use RDLR as a counter when they try to knee slice pass
Practice inverting drills daily to build comfort upside down
Combine RDLR with standard DLR for a complete guard system
Learn to transition to deep half when they pressure you flat
Use collar and sleeve grips to prevent them establishing crossface
Chain RDLR with kiss of the dragon for back takes
Don't stay flat on your back — angle your body toward the hook side

guards.name: Collar Sleeve Guard 👔
guards.description: Collar Sleeve Guard is an open guard position where you control your opponent using a cross collar grip and a same-side sleeve grip. Combined with feet on the hips or biceps, it offers excellent distance management and creates a wide variety of sweeps, submissions, and transitions. It is a staple of modern Gi guard play.
execution: Start from open guard with your opponent standing or on their knees
Establish a cross collar grip — reach across and grip their far collar deep
Grip their same-side sleeve with your other hand
Place one foot on their same-side hip for distance control
Place your other foot on their bicep (sleeve grip side) to extend them
Keep your hips angled toward the collar grip side
Use push-pull tension between your grips and feet constantly
Stay mobile — never let your back flatten to the mat
Be ready to transition to triangle, omoplata, or sweeps at any moment
pros: Excellent distance management with feet and grips working together
High submission rate — triangle and omoplata entries are very accessible
Powerful sweeps when opponent tries to pass or disengage
Works well against standing and kneeling opponents
Great for controlling aggressive passers
Pairs naturally with spider, lasso, and DLR guards
One of the most complete attacking guard systems in Gi
cons: Requires Gi — not applicable in No-Gi
Grip fighting is critical — broken grips leave you vulnerable
Demands strong grip endurance over long matches
Can be passed with leg drags if feet are out of position
Vulnerable to stack passes when feet are on biceps
Requires consistent hip movement to stay effective
Difficult to maintain against very aggressive pressure passers
tips: The cross collar grip is king — get it deep and never let go
Always have at least one foot active — hip or bicep
Use the sleeve grip to prevent them passing to that side
Set up the triangle by pulling sleeve across and shooting hips up
Chain omoplata with triangle — they defend one, go to the other
Study Leandro Lo's collar sleeve game — he was the modern master
Practice re-gripping quickly when grips are broken
Use the collar grip to pull them into your attacks, not just hold
Angle your body to the collar grip side for better attack angles
Combine collar sleeve with lasso on the sleeve side for hybrid guard

guards.name: Z-Guard (Knee Shield) 🛡️
guards.description: Z-Guard is a half guard variation where you frame a knee shield across your opponent's torso, creating distance and preventing them from applying pressure. Your bottom leg maintains the half guard hook while the top knee acts as a barrier. It is one of the most effective defensive and offensive half guard positions in modern BJJ.
execution: Start from standard half guard with one of their legs trapped
Bring your top knee across their torso as a frame (the knee shield)
Angle your shin diagonally across their chest or midsection
Keep your bottom leg hooking their trapped leg to maintain half guard
Grip their collar with your top hand (Gi) or frame on their neck/shoulder (No-Gi)
Use your bottom hand to control their far sleeve, wrist, or underhook
Maintain distance — the knee shield prevents them from closing space
Keep your hips angled toward the knee shield side, not flat
Stay active with grips and ready to transition to sweeps or submissions
pros: Excellent distance management with the knee shield frame
Prevents opponent from applying crushing top pressure
Highly versatile — sweeps, submissions, and transitions available
Works extremely well in both Gi and No-Gi
Great recovery position when guard is partially passed
Allows you to rest while maintaining an active defensive frame
Easy to learn and effective at all belt levels
cons: Can be collapsed if opponent gets past the knee shield
Vulnerable to knee slice passes that split the shield
Requires active grip fighting to prevent opponent from settling
Can be smashed if opponent drops weight on the shield knee
Less effective if your hips get flattened to the mat
Opponent can backstep around the shield to pass
Requires constant adjustment as opponent changes angles
tips: Keep your knee shield high across their chest — not on the belly
Never let your hips go flat — angle toward the shield side always
Use the underhook on the far side to set up sweeps and back takes
Study Craig Jones and Bernardo Faria for elite Z-guard systems
Chain Z-guard with deep half — if they pass the shield, go deep
Practice the arm drag from Z-guard for back takes
Use your knee shield foot to push off their hip for re-framing
Combine with kimura grip attacks for powerful submission chains
Don't just defend — use Z-guard offensively to sweep and submit
Learn to transition to full guard, butterfly, or DLR when needed

guards.name: Octopus Guard 🐙
guards.description: Octopus Guard is a unique half guard variation where you sit up into your opponent, wrap around their body like an octopus, and use an overhook combined with a deep underhook to off-balance and sweep. Popularized by Eduardo Telles, it turns a seemingly disadvantageous position into a highly offensive one by clinging tightly to the opponent and using their momentum against them.
execution: Start from half guard with your opponent in top position
Sit up into your opponent and get chest-to-chest contact
Establish an overhook on one of their arms — lock it tight
Use your other arm to get a deep underhook on the opposite side
Wrap your body around theirs — stay glued with no space
Keep your half guard hook on the overhook side
Drive your head into their armpit or shoulder on the underhook side
Use your body weight and grips to off-balance them laterally
Stay active — use constant pressure and angle changes to set up sweeps
pros: Turns a defensive half guard into a highly offensive position
Excellent for taking the back with the deep underhook
Neutralizes opponent's top pressure by sitting up into them
Works well against larger and stronger opponents
Creates powerful sweeping angles from an unexpected position
Effective in both Gi and No-Gi
Difficult for opponent to disengage once you are attached
cons: Requires good timing to sit up without getting flattened
Vulnerable to d'arce and guillotine chokes during entry
Can be difficult to maintain if opponent sprawls hard
Requires strong grip endurance to control overhook and underhook
Less effective if you don't fully commit to sitting up
Opponent can use whizzer to counter the underhook
Relatively niche position — less drilling partners will be familiar with it
tips: Commit fully when sitting up — hesitation gets you flattened
The overhook is the anchor — never let it go once established
Study Eduardo Telles for the original octopus guard system
Use your head as a post against their body for leverage
Chain with back takes — the underhook is already halfway there
Practice the sit-up motion from half guard until it becomes reflex
Keep zero space between your body and theirs — cling like an octopus
Use their reactions to your advantage — if they push, sweep; if they pull, take the back
Combine octopus guard with dogfight position for more options
Protect your neck during the sit-up to avoid guillotine and d'arce

guards.name: Worm Guard 🪱
guards.description: Worm Guard is an innovative lapel-based open guard created by Keenan Cornelius. It involves threading your opponent's own lapel around their leg and gripping it from underneath, creating a powerful tether that controls their movement and prevents guard passing. It is one of the most dominant modern Gi guard systems and has spawned an entire family of lapel guard variations.
execution: Start from open guard — De La Riva or seated guard works well
Pull out your opponent's far-side lapel and feed it to your hand
Thread the lapel behind and around their lead leg
Grip the lapel with your hand that comes underneath their leg
Establish a De La Riva hook on the same leg for added control
Keep tension on the lapel — it should be taut at all times
Use your free hand to control their sleeve or collar
Keep your hips angled and mobile — never flat on your back
Use the lapel tether to prevent them from stepping back or disengaging
pros: Extremely difficult to pass once fully established
Creates a physical tether that limits opponent's movement
Powerful sweep options with the lapel controlling their base
Allows transitions to squid guard, ringworm, and other lapel guards
Excellent for controlling distance and shutting down passing
Gives you time to set up attacks while opponent is stuck
Highly effective at the highest levels of Gi competition
cons: Only works in the Gi — completely inapplicable in No-Gi
Requires time and opportunity to feed and thread the lapel
Can be shut down early if opponent controls their own lapel
Steep learning curve with many specific grip sequences
Referee may restart if position stalls for too long
Less effective if opponent knows the specific counters
Requires a loose-fitting gi to pull the lapel out
tips: Speed on the lapel feed is critical — do it before they react
Study Keenan Cornelius extensively — he invented and perfected this system
Always maintain tension on the lapel — slack kills the position
Combine worm guard with DLR hook for maximum control
Learn the lapel lasso and squid guard as companion positions
Practice transitioning between worm guard variations fluidly
Use worm guard sweeps to accumulate advantages in competition
If they try to untie the lapel, attack immediately while they're distracted
Keep your free hand active — collar or sleeve grip adds layers of control
Don't rely only on the lapel — use your legs and hips actively too

guards.name: K Guard 🔑
guards.description: K Guard is a modern leg entanglement entry position created by Lachlan Giles. From a seated or supine guard, you frame one shin across the opponent's thigh while your other leg threads behind their far leg, forming a 'K' shape. It is designed primarily as a gateway into heel hooks, knee bars, and other leg lock positions, and has become a staple of contemporary No-Gi grappling.
execution: Start from seated guard, butterfly guard, or open guard
Frame your near-side shin across your opponent's lead thigh
Thread your far-side leg behind their far leg at the knee level
Your legs form a 'K' shape — one blocking, one threading
Grip their near-side ankle or heel for control
Use your hands to frame on their shoulders or chest for distance
Keep your hips angled and mobile — stay off your back
Use the position to off-balance and enter leg entanglements
Be ready to transition to inside sankaku, 50/50, or outside ashi
pros: Elite entry system into heel hooks and leg locks
Works extremely well in No-Gi grappling
Difficult for opponent to see the entries coming
Allows multiple leg lock entries from one position
Great for smaller grapplers using technique over strength
Pairs well with butterfly and seated guard systems
Low risk when performed correctly — easy to bail and reset
cons: Steep learning curve — requires understanding of leg lock systems
Less effective in Gi where opponent has more grips to counter
Can be countered by experienced leg lockers who know the entries
Requires good timing — too slow and opponent can smash past
Limited sweep options compared to traditional guards
Exposes your own legs to counter leg lock attacks
Relatively new system — less instructional material available
tips: Study Lachlan Giles — he created and perfected the K Guard system
Master the inside sankaku entry — it's the highest percentage attack
Use K Guard as a surprise entry — don't telegraph your intentions
Drill the transition from butterfly guard to K Guard until seamless
Learn all three main entries: inside sankaku, outside ashi, 50/50
Keep your framing shin heavy on their thigh to control distance
Practice the backside 50/50 entry for when they step back
Combine K Guard with standard sweeps for a complete game plan
Don't stay in K Guard too long — enter, attack, or transition
Study the Lachlan Giles vs. ADCC 2019 absolute division for application

guards.name: 50/50 Guard ⚖️
guards.description: 50/50 Guard is a symmetrical leg entanglement position where both players have identical hooks on each other's legs, mirroring one another. Each player threads one leg inside and wraps around the opponent's opposite leg, creating an even or 'fifty-fifty' position. It is a cornerstone of modern leg lock systems and is also used strategically for sweeps and advantages in Gi competition.
execution: Start from open guard, single leg X, K Guard, or a scramble
Thread your inside leg between your opponent's legs
Wrap your leg around their far leg from the outside
Hook your foot behind their knee or thigh on the far side
Your opponent will have a mirrored hook on your leg
Control their far ankle or heel with your hands
Keep your hips close to their leg and stay tight
Use your free leg and arms to maintain balance and control
Stay on your side or slightly elevated — never flat on your back
pros: Foundation of modern heel hook and leg lock systems
Symmetrical position offers equal attacking opportunities
Works extremely well in No-Gi grappling and ADCC rules
Excellent for accumulating advantages in Gi competition (sweeps)
Difficult for opponent to disengage once entangled
Creates a 50/50 submission race that rewards technique
Natural transition point from many guards (DLR, K Guard, SLX)
cons: Can lead to stalling matches — both players may be stuck
Symmetrical nature means your opponent has equal attacks
Penalized or disliked in some Gi rulesets for stalling potential
Requires strong leg lock knowledge to be effective offensively
Can be difficult to sweep from without leg lock threat
Exposes your own legs to counter attacks at all times
Can become a positional stalemate without finishing ability
tips: Study Gordon Ryan and the Danaher Death Squad for elite 50/50 systems
The inside position (inside sankaku) is more dominant — fight for it
Always control your opponent's heel or ankle — never let it float
Use the 50/50 sweep to come on top and then attack leg locks
Learn the honey hole / inside sankaku transition from 50/50
Practice escaping 50/50 as much as attacking — it goes both ways
Use the backside 50/50 for a more dominant heel hook angle
In Gi, use 50/50 sweeps to score advantages and points strategically
Don't just sit in 50/50 — actively work to improve your angle
Chain heel hook attacks with toe holds and knee bars from 50/50
